title: Apple Intelligence approved for launch in China with Alibaba and Baidu
content: Apple Intelligence, Apple's generative AI offering, is finally coming to China after months of regulatory delays. On July 15, 2026, China's internet content regulator, the Cyberspace Administration of China, approved Apple's AI services in the country based on a deal to integrate Alibaba's Qwen AI model into Apple's operating systems, including iOS, iPadOS, macOS, and visionOS. A Baidu spokesperson subsequently confirmed to TechCrunch that Baidu is also working with Apple on developing Apple Intelligence features for Chinese users. The Alibaba deal, which had been rumored since early 2025, marks a critical milestone for Apple's AI ambitions in a key market. In the second quarter of 2026, Apple generated $20.5 billion in sales in Greater China, up 28% from a year earlier, and recently reclaimed its No. 2 position in China's smartphone market following aggressive iPhone discounts during shopping festivals. Apple is also reportedly exploring integrations with DeepSeek and ByteDance to further diversify its AI partnerships in the region. Alibaba confirmed the news in a statement to CNBC, noting that its Qwen models would be integrated into Apple Intelligence experiences, though no specific timeline was provided. The approval ends a long wait for Apple Intelligence features, which first debuted in 2024 but were held back from Chinese users due to regulatory hurdles around generative AI services.

title: Moonshot's upcoming Kimi 3 is expected to close the gap with Anthropic's Opus 4.8
content: Chinese AI lab Moonshot AI is preparing to release Kimi K3, the next iteration of its Kimi model series, which is expected to perform at par with or even surpass Anthropic's Opus 4.8, according to a Financial Times report citing anonymous sources. Moonshot's existing Kimi K2 models have already gained strong traction in the open-source AI market, ranking highly on benchmarks and demonstrating capabilities not far behind the latest frontier models from leading U.S. AI labs. The upcoming Kimi K3 is said to be the largest open-weight AI model from China, with a parameter count between 2 trillion and 3 trillion, and is expected to be released within days. Industry observers see the release as a significant step toward closing the gap between China's open-source AI models and the closed-source frontier models from OpenAI and Anthropic. Moonshot is also said to be raising fresh capital in a round that would value the company at $31.5 billion, building on the $2 billion it raised in May 2026 at a $20 billion valuation. The news arrives amid a broader debate about the value of expensive closed-source AI models, with industry leaders concerned about data privacy when submitting client information to AI labs like OpenAI and Anthropic. Executives are increasingly pitching open-source alternatives from labs like DeepSeek, Z.ai, and Moonshot as cost-effective options that companies can train on their own data.

title: Why AMI Labs' Alexandre LeBrun won't call his AI 'AGI' or 'superintelligence'
content: While much of the AI industry races to label its work as "AGI" or "superintelligence," Alexandre LeBrun, the CEO of AMI Labs — the world model startup backed by Yann LeCun — takes a deliberately different stance. In an interview with TechCrunch, LeBrun said his company avoids both terms entirely, dismissing them as vague and ultimately meaningless marketing labels. "We never used the word AGI. And I just noticed that nobody is using it anymore; they switched to superintelligence," he said. "Next time we'll switch to something else." LeBrun, speaking from Seoul where he was attending the International Conference on Machine Learning to scout for industrial partners and researchers, said AMI Labs is actively courting robotics, manufacturing, and electronics companies, even though the company remains pre-product. A world model incorporates physics to predict and interact with the real world, a fundamentally different approach from language models that predict the next word. LeBrun argued that AI remains "really dumb in the physical world" and that robots are currently running fixed, static routines. Even modest gains in physical-world awareness could represent major breakthroughs, he said, pointing to a recent incident where a robot performing kung fu at a public event kicked a child — an outcome a context-aware AI might have prevented. The robotics sector is seen as one of the largest potential applications for world models, though LeBrun noted that hardware has advanced dramatically in recent months while the intelligence layer remains the bottleneck.

title: How a former DeepMind researcher raised at a $300M pre-seed valuation before launching a product
content: Andrew Dai, a former Google DeepMind researcher, has founded Elorian and pulled off what may be one of the most aggressive early-stage AI fundraises in recent memory. Just months after leaving Google, Dai raised a $55 million seed round at a $300 million valuation — a ratio that rivals some of the largest seed rounds in U.S. history, including those raised by Thinking Machines Lab. In an episode of TechCrunch's Build Mode podcast, Dai explained that visual AI is the frontier he believes will define the next phase of artificial intelligence. "You have models that are doing really great at math, really great at new physics ideas, and of course coding is very popular now," he said. "But one area where progress has been extremely uneven is visual understanding and visual reasoning. At Elorian, we want to build models that will advance us toward visual AGI." Dai walked through the fundraising process from the founder's perspective, detailing how he distilled a highly technical research vision into a compelling narrative for investors. Notably, he prioritized strategic investors like Nvidia and Menlo Ventures over other offers that carried higher valuations, believing that partners who deeply understood the realities of building frontier AI would prove more valuable in the long run. The episode also covers practical lessons for founders navigating today's fast-moving AI landscape, including how to communicate complex technical concepts without jargon, why speed has become a primary competitive advantage, and what it takes to recruit top researchers away from well-resourced tech giants.

title: Why is OpenAI selling a ChatGPT basketball?
content: OpenAI this week released its first piece of hardware — a $230 mini keyboard for its Codex coding agent — but the company also quietly dropped another product that caught more than a few eyes: a ChatGPT basketball priced at $70. The ball is part of what OpenAI calls the "Pause. Play. Prompt." campaign, described on the product listing as "a physical reminder that creativity doesn't just live on our screens." The basketball is made of 100% rubber, making it better suited for outdoor play and weather-resistant compared to professional leather basketballs. It remains unclear who the target customer for a ChatGPT-branded basketball actually is, though the humor of bringing an AI-logo ball to a public basketball court has not gone unnoticed. The product joins a history of tech companies launching unusual merchandise, though the AI industry is not particularly known for its product-market fit instincts — the Humane Ai Pin being a frequently cited example of a high-profile AI hardware failure. OpenAI also released other items alongside the basketball, continuing a pattern of branded merchandise drops that blend genuine utility with novelty marketing. The timing is notable given ongoing concerns about generative AI's environmental footprint, as tech companies face increasing scrutiny over the carbon emissions driven by AI data center expansion. Whether the ChatGPT basketball represents clever branding, genuine brand extension, or something closer to ironic conference swag remains a matter of perspective.

title: Yes, you can now order DoorDash from the command line
content: DoorDash has introduced DoorDash CLI, a command-line tool for developers that enables AI agents to order food, search stores, find deals, and complete checkout directly through the platform. Called "dd-cli," the tool is currently in limited beta and open to U.S. and Canadian macOS developers via a waitlist, DoorDash co-founder and CTO Andy Fang announced on X. On the surface, the idea of ordering lunch via a terminal command may seem absurd — command-line tools are traditionally associated with programming, not food delivery. But DoorDash CLI is less a novelty and more an example of what "agentic commerce" looks like in practice: by exposing its ordering platform to AI agents, DoorDash allows developers to embed food ordering capabilities into their own software and services. Instead of opening the DoorDash app, users could interact with an AI agent that handles the entire ordering process on their behalf. DoorDash has been experimenting with AI-driven commerce for some time, including an iMessage integration and its own AI chatbot called "Ask DoorDash," which lets users order using prompts and photos. The DoorDash CLI takes this a step further by giving developers programmatic access to the platform, potentially enabling a wide range of agentic use cases — from automated lunch ordering for office teams to integration with other productivity tools. The move positions DoorDash as an infrastructure provider for the emerging AI agent ecosystem.

title: Google's AI Mode now lets you link and interact with select apps
content: Google announced on July 16, 2026 that AI Mode, its conversational AI search experience, now allows users to link and interact with select third-party apps directly within the interface. At launch, supported apps include Instacart, Canva, and YouTube. The update expands AI Mode from answering questions to completing tasks across the apps users already rely on daily. In one example, Google showed how a user planning a barbecue could build a grocery list in AI Mode and then connect their Instacart account to add ingredients directly to their shopping cart and check out without leaving the Google ecosystem. Another demo showed a user asking Canva to surface design templates for a flyer directly within AI Mode, and a scenario where a user could curate a YouTube Music playlist and save it instantly. The rollout is initially limited to U.S. users, with Google saying it is working with additional partners to expand the app roster soon. Today's update builds on a capability Google launched earlier in 2026 that lets users connect third-party apps to the Gemini app, with supported integrations including Canva, OpenTable, Spark, and Instacart. The move puts Google in direct competition with rivals like OpenAI's ChatGPT and Anthropic's Claude, both of which already support app integrations. Since AI Mode's launch in early 2025, Google has been rapidly expanding its capabilities, most recently adding a feature that helps users check whether items are in stock at nearby stores.

title: Oil giant BP shutters its corporate venture arm after 20 years
content: BP has announced it is selling the majority of its venture portfolio — comprising more than 10 companies — to Verdane, a Nordic private equity firm, effectively shutting down its corporate venture arm after 20 years of operation. The move comes on top of BP's earlier pivot away from clean energy initiatives and represents a full retreat from its previous commitment to the energy transition. Since launching BP Ventures in 2007, the company had invested across a wide range of sectors including green hydrogen, e-mobility, ride-hailing, autonomous vehicles, private jet charters, and geothermal energy. BP said it would retain interests in a small number of investments where the technology has potential to create value for its core businesses, but declined to specify which companies would be kept. The company also declined to comment on the fate of BP Ventures employees, though layoffs appear likely. The portfolio sale is expected to close in the second quarter of 2027. BP's venture arm had a mixed financial track record, with its diversified bets across mobility and energy transition startups not generating the returns hoped for. The closure marks another signal of major oil majors scaling back climate-related investments, a trend that has accelerated as crude oil prices remain elevated and clean energy economics face ongoing challenges. BP follows other oil giants in reassessing its venture strategy amid pressure to focus on core fossil fuel operations.

title: Roblox launches an AI-powered game-creation feature in its mobile app
content: Roblox announced a new feature called "Build" that allows users to design and generate basic games directly from their mobile devices using AI, with no programming experience required. The feature transforms simple text prompts — for example, "Let's make a cozy adventure game set in a dense forest" — into an initial playable game that users can then modify and share with friends. According to Roblox, Build is powered by a broad set of AI models including both open-source and proprietary Roblox models, handling gameplay mechanics, environment design, characters, visual style, sound, and more. The launch puts Roblox in competition with similar tools from Google, Microsoft, and Tencent that aim to lower the barriers to game creation. However, AI-powered game generation has raised concerns within the developer community. A Game Developers Conference survey found that 52% of game industry professionals believe generative AI is having a negative impact on the industry, with critics arguing that easy AI-generated games could flood platforms with low-quality content and increase competition for human creators. Roblox says it will rank AI-generated games based on player retention — if a game is not played or engaged with, it will not be prominently featured. The company insists its discovery systems are designed to surface games with long-term retention and filter out AI slop, though creators remain cautious about what a democratized creation pipeline means for the quality and diversity of content on the platform.

title: Google Vids now lets you star in your own AI videos
content: Google announced an update to Google Vids that allows users to create a custom digital avatar that looks and sounds like them, generated from a selfie and a voice recording they upload. The feature is powered by Google's Gemini Omni multi-modal AI model, which can combine written prompts, reference images, and other inputs to produce AI-generated video content. Gemini Omni in Vids can also be used to swap out video backgrounds, adjust lighting, add visual effects, or make step-by-step edits without starting over from scratch. The update transforms Google Vids — originally positioned as an AI-assisted workplace presentation tool — into a more comprehensive AI video creation platform. By integrating Vids into Google Workspace, Google is clearly targeting business use cases like company updates, training videos, and internal communications, while the personalized avatar feature introduces a consumer-facing dimension that brings it into competition with AI video startups like HeyGen, Synthesia, Captions, and D-ID. Google notes that AI avatars will be tied to the account holder's likeness and subject to usage policies designed to prevent misuse. The timing is notable given that OpenAI's Sora video generation tool recently shut down, suggesting the market for AI video tools is still evolving and that Google's integration of the technology into an existing productivity suite may offer a more accessible on-ramp for enterprise users compared to standalone AI video products.
